content.text: The Möbius function enables us to "invert" summations over divisors.

content.title: Möbius Function
content.content:
The **Möbius function** $\mu(n)$ is defined by:
\[
\mu(n) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } n = 1 \\
(-1)^k & \text{if } n = p_1 p_2 \cdots p_k \text{ (distinct primes)} \\
0 & \text{if } p^2 \mid n \text{ for some prime } p
\end{cases}
\]

For $n = 1$: $\sum_{d \mid 1} \mu(d) = \mu(1) = 1$.

For $n = p_1^{e_1} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$ with $k \geq 1$: terms with $\mu(d) \neq 0$ 
correspond to square-free divisors. These are products of subsets of 
$\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$:
\[
\sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \binom{k}{j}(-1)^j = (1 - 1)^k = 0. \qedhere
\]

content.title: Möbius Inversion Formula
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are arithmetic functions, then
\[
g(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} f(d) \quad \text{for all } n
\]
if and only if
\[
f(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) g(n/d) = \sum_{d \mid n} \mu(n/d) g(d) \quad \text{for all } n.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Assuming $g(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} f(d)$:
$$
\sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) g(n/d) &= \sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) \sum_{e \mid (n/d)} f(e) \\
&= \sum_{e \mid n} f(e) \sum_{d \mid (n/e)} \mu(d) \\
&= \sum_{e \mid n} f(e) [n/e = 1] = f(n).
$$
The converse is similar.

content.title: Recovering $\phi$
content.problem:
Since $\sum_{d \mid n} \phi(d) = n$, by Möbius inversion:
\[
\phi(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) \cdot \frac{n}{d} = n \sum_{d \mid n} \frac{\mu(d)}{d}.
\]
